From starting to scratch, not knowing anything about the system or anything about it automatically, you know, to be at the top of what your niche is. I've been at the top of that niche for three and a half years as well. We were at the top right from day one. The thing is my advantage is I don't know anything. I still don't. I just, you know, I started at my kitchen table. Now I've got almost 4,000 square feet of office space with employees and video studios and everything else, all in three and a half years. But the thing is, I think there's a huge disadvantage to people who know stuff, because then they feel like they got to do it all. And there's not a whole lot that you need to do. You know, you get so many people, you know, I just spoke at, well, just a few months ago, spoke at the traffic and conversion summit, and they're learning 5,000 things to do. I'm really blessed to be dumb because I can't do 5,000 things. I can do one thing at a time and I focus on one thing at a time. And if anybody's listening to this that wants to know what my advice is, find one thing, do it well, get it going, get it successful, then move on to the second thing. And that's what we've done. And I think that's why we've been very successful. Have there been anything that you did that was like a big leverage point where you go? We're going along, we're doing this click bank thing, and then we did something different that pulled us ahead of the pack, that of all the other people in Unish. What I do with any business is I do a diagram of my business, you know, a CEO, and then I work down to everything else. And in the beginning, you're doing everything yourself. So the most important thing I did was immediately hire, as soon as I had a couple of bucks come in, I immediately hired somebody. So that person took things off of my place so I could focus on the things more important than I hired the second person. And now I only focus on two or three things on a daily basis or weekly or monthly basis. As far as the marketing goes, my natural personality, I'm a good networker. I wanted to get to know everybody I could in this arena, and I did very quickly. So for me, we focused on affiliate marketing. Now we're getting into other things, media buying, and I've been working with a PR company for the last few years. Well, I've been, I've been very fortunate. I haven't had to worry about that because when they promote for me, they make good money. Um, I know there's rankings in click bank and then there's earnings per click and there's conversion rates and all this kind of stuff. Me personally, I, I've got a, I've got a, um, spreadsheet that I look at every day. We're actually building an executive dashboard right now that will be done hopefully in the next few weeks. But right now I just look at a spreadsheet that shows my earnings. Uh, I'm more interested in my earnings than I am conversions and all those other stuff. I'm a businessman. Uh, I'm not an internet marketer. Internet marketers look at all this other stuff. Uh, I think you can get very confusing because you can have convert, you can have very, very high conversions. Um, and I see this on a lot of launches will do. Oh, the conversions were at 8% in, but our earnings were in the toilet. You know, I don't care how well it converts. If it's not making me money, I want to make money. So, um, when people promote from me, we've been very fortunate. I've got two of the top, two, two warfers in the top five, um, on click bank. And they both make our affiliates money. So, uh, I think that's the bottom line. The better affiliates are more interested in their money than they are in their conversions because they want to stay in business and be in business. And, um, I would imagine that you're probably good at very decision making. Like there's some risk involved. You're willing to risk, you're willing to, to not, you know, have something done, but you're going to, but you're not going to dwell on it. This is my guess, but when you decide on something, you just go for it full force, even if it will or will not turn out. I have never entered a business venture that I thought I was going to fail in. Really? And I, oh yeah, I read everything. I'm 100% I'm convinced that it'll be a success. That being said, I've gone broke three times work, work, you know, um, which I find most successful people have done. Um, yes, I can make decisions. And that was what I was the youngest senior VP in Manhattan in real estate. I worked for the Helmsley's as well as other people. That was one of the things that separated me from everybody else. I was able to make a decision. People with people with business degrees, master's degrees, attorneys and accounts, they just, they had to tell you, no, because they're not capable of making a decision. I was able to make a decision and then suffer the consequences and, and deal with the victories as well. So, so yeah, you're not going to succeed if you don't believe it the whole time, even though sometimes you won't, you just have to go with that attitude. Guns blazing all the way. Yeah. Make a decision, work your ass off towards it, do what you can do. And then also, I'm also very good at cutting my losses. You know, if something's not working, let's, you know, I'm good at making that decision too. I don't hang on to something like it's one of my children. I hang on to my children like that and my grandchildren and my family. When it comes to business, I, I make, I make decisions quickly. And, uh, if something's not making money or if it looks like it's going the wrong way, you know, it's time to cut your losses. I have no problem doing that. To me, like a lot of the stuff that we're talking about, um, you know, our father had to sing all the time that we grew up with called motion over meditation. You know, get up and move. I like that. Don't sit around and think about it. You just want to, you know, get up. It's better to make moves, you know, than, than to sit around and think about what you're planning on doing for a year. And don't get it perfect. Get it going. Get it going. 80, 80% is fine. I talk to so many people that, you know, I've had a lot of people helping me. So if somebody gets through to me, which I try to make it a little more difficult these days, but if somebody gets through to me, I've got no problem talking to them. Most of the people that I speak to have been at this for 10 years. They've been every mastermind that they've been at every marketing thing. They've taken every program. They read everything and have done nothing. And it doesn't take all that, you know, do something and just get going, man. Most people, they're so busy getting ready to get ready that they never do anything. Exactly. I always say that they fail. Anything worth doing is worth doing poorly. Exactly. Then learning get going. Man, when I started, I didn't know how to make a PDF, but I was out there promoting myself to these affiliates and busting my ass and making believe I knew what I was doing. I faked it till I made it, baby. But we made it. Yeah. And you know, the other thing, I still don't know how to make a PDF, by the way. Well, but you know, the other thing though, I like that you're also resonating with this resonating is you focus on being good at the one thing that you're trying to focus on at the moment. Focus on one thing at a time, be good at it. Just try and be a little better than you were yesterday at it. And you know, everything else falls in line. So if you worry about creating the very best product there is, you know, it's easier to sell eventually. You know, if you, if your main focus is managing your team and you become, you know, really good at motivating and managing, that can be, you know, the driving spark. And so yeah, motion over meditation and, you know, do well, but don't be afraid not to do well. Yeah. Right now in the beginning, obviously I did everything, but right now I handle about 15 to 20% of a project. I handle the first 10% and I handle the last 10%. And everything else in the middle gets done by my team, whether it's creating a digital product or whatever we're doing. So I'll, I'll get it going. I'll let them finish it. I'll review it, fine tune it. And then when it's time for marketing, it goes to my company. Like right, we did the E factor. We've been working on the E factor for the past year or so between creating the product and testing and everything else. Um, I don't look at that now. That's in my funnel. That's in my, that's in my team's hands. I don't look at that. If you're old school, the body, I don't look at that. That's with my team. I'm on, I'm on to the new thing. How long were you into this doing it all yourself? And, you know, how much was that like, uh, like an anchor holding you back? And then all of a sudden, like you, did you waste, you know, 10 years of your life trying to do everything yourself all of a sudden you figured out you need to do the 15% and all of a sudden the shackles came off and you bolted out or soon as I had the money, I hired some of my, I have my first person, which was my son. Um, I always surround myself with very, very small people and people who are good at what they do. I know I don't know it all. I don't want to know it all. I just want to do what I'm good at, but in the beginning, you do need to do it yourself. So probably the first year I did everything myself. Second year I brought my son on and he did customer service. And then, uh, he worked his way into being the affiliate manager and handling all lists. Now he's president of that company. So the second year he started freeing me up. Third year we had a full team in place and, and then the third year I just do what I do now. You know, it, it, it refines itself every couple of months. I work with Dan Sullivan as a, as a coach. Uh, we're refining it still, but, um, the ultimate goal is to have the company be the company and me do what I do best. And we're almost there right now. We're probably 80% there now, 80 to 90%. Okay. Cool. That's great. Um, I have a question for you myself. If I might, uh, we've talked about, you mentioned a couple of your products and we know that you're massively successful. Uh, what I would like to know is what are all of the products that you have out there on ClickBank in case somebody wants to become an affiliate. Okay. We've got UX3, which is my supplement. Um, it's a supplement on ClickBank. That's white listed. So you need to contact my son, Jim, uh, Jim and JohnRolly.net. And, uh, in order to get on that, we've got old school, new body. And then we've got the E-Factor diet. They're very easy to find. They're both in the top five of, um, ClickBank. So, um, just go on there and we'd love to have you promote it, um, especially if you're promoting ethically. And, uh, so it's easy to find. But, uh, I want to point out something. I've really been blessed. I've got a lot of really, really good people around me. John Benson writes my copy. He's done a phenomenal job. Um, and I also want to throw something else in here. Um, yesterday I had a over an hour long conversation with my team and Bond. Um, Bond and Kevin are so successful because they're out there and they work hard. They help others. Never once did Bond ask me for a retainer, ask me for any money. Yet he brought me probably a million dollars worth of value. We're changing things today based on what Bond told us yesterday. So they're very, very selfless. Both of them. Kevin has been on the phone with me as well helping me. So anybody who's listening to this, I just want you to know they're in business. They're in it for the money, but they're really not in it for the money. They're into it because they really like helping people. And please don't bill me now. Not if I said that. John, I just want to ask you, I take a slightly different direction a little bit. A big part of your philosophy seems to be this physical, mental and spiritual. Yes. You know, do you think that, you know, the physical is quite, uh, or easier to identify and how you can work that because you can train and you can eat and you can do everything else. But the spiritual and the mental part, do you have like affirmations or do you have a kind of disciplined habit of how you develop the mental side and the spiritual side? Is that an active process? Yeah, it is an active process. I've been at this for a long time as far as the mental. And I'm very deliberate. You know, I make sure that I surround myself with, with smart people, motivated people. I try to keep negative people away from myself. I read, I read every day. I stay on top of things. And I try to keep a, I try to keep a positive outlook all the time. I get frustrated sometimes with things, but I, I'm quick to not be frustrated. I'm quick to apologize if I get frustrated with somebody, especially one of my employees, really anybody. Um, spiritually, I'm a Christian, so my basis there is the Bible. So I get into the Bible every morning. I'm not here to evangelize, but that's what I do. And, um, so my book, The Power of Positive Fitness is a mental, physical and spiritual book with a Christian philosophy. It was, it's actually, it was, um, if you go into any Christian bookstores, usually on a, on an end cap, uh, old school, new body is a, is a secular book. And I did not, I don't want to be clear. I did not want to do a Christian book. My, my agent was Rick Warren's agent and they kind of talked me into it. I'm an author that happens to be a Christian, but I'm not a Christian author. I don't want to, I'm not Billy Graham. I don't want anybody judging God based on the way I live my life. So, but so yeah, so I do have, I do have some personal habits. I get up very early. I do cardio while I'm doing cardio. I typically watch, um, a podcast either on marketing or self-help or a Christian podcast. Then I get my Bible for a while, pray, eat and get on with my day. So I get up early and I try to have the first hour and a half just for me. So it's, uh, let me just, so you have your antenna up for new ideas for what you're doing with these podcasts, to take you're not searching for something in particular, you're looking for things that come in as inspiration or something. But also I wanted to ask you, when you're exercising, do you have a lot of epiphanies while you're doing that morning ritual? Oh yeah. That's when I get my best ideas. That one, I'm in the shower, you know, it's, or if I go for a walk, I, you know, we've got an office complex here. I sell them. The only time I sit at my desk talking like this is if I'm doing something like this. Um, I typically take myself on, put my headset on, if I'm doing a conference school, speaking to somebody and I walk, I constantly move. I work at an ups as a standup desk most of the time. Um, so my physiology is always engaged. And I learned early on that your physiology affects your psychology. So people who are sitting in a chair, you know, slumped over and the, you know, their mind is going to follow that. So to answer Mark's question, you know, physical fitness to me is very, very important because your, your, your, your physiology affects not only your psychology, but also your spiritual life. So you can't be, you can't be out of shape physically and expect to be your sharpest mentally or your sharpest spiritually because your mind and spirit are housed in your body. So if you've got a fat, sloppy body that's exhausted, the rest of you is going to be exhausted all the time. I don't mean to be so carpeted. It's true. If you're, if you, if you're out of shape and you tie it all the time, the same blood that runs through your brain body runs through your bank. President John F. Kennedy once said that physical fitness is not only the key to a healthy body, but it's the basis of dynamic and creative intellectual activity. So I think if you want to be a really good thinker, you need to get into the gym and be a bit of a stinker, you know, the nice thing. You haven't, sorry, Bonn, I just want to, well, there's not so much that I know that there's a lot of writers and copywriters who are on the call and part of the group, including Christine. And I know that some of them are, we would be remiss if we didn't explore the author side of you, John. So Christine, I'm going to give you that question to ask John. We can't hear you though. Still can't hear you. I don't know what's going on with this. You mute yourself and you forget. You're muted. And you're, yeah. So, yeah, I am a working copywriter in case you guys didn't realize that. I don't just sit here and talk to people on the internet. What I am sure that a lot of the folks in there are curious about is the same thing that I am. I, can you share what drove you to start writing and what your writing process is? Sure. I always wanted to be a writer since I was young. I was a D student. In fact, I was told not even to go to high school, but I was marginally retarded. So, yeah, that's the guys honest truth. And we wanted to be right. I never really did it. And then one day, Barbara Corkman called me up. She's in the shock tank. She's a very close friend of mine. She goes, you need to write a book. And I was like, you know, nobody cares about me anymore. I'm down in North Carolina. I'm not Manhattan real estate anymore. And we talked. So the basis of my first book was, you know, course called Climulant or success without running out of the gas. And basically how physical fitness helped me, you know, and the tools, how it helped me in the business world. You know, I was 230 pounds, solid rock muscle, very little body fat in my late twenties and early thirties in Manhattan real estate. I used that to my advantage. Everybody was fat salobs that I worked with. They were all 20 and 30 years old of them. I was by far the youngest senior VP. As soon as I walked into a meeting, I take my jacket off and I had the suspenders on back then, my tie and people to see my muscles coming through. And I didn't do it to intimidate people, but people respect people who look good. Whether you like it or not. People respect you if you look strong. And I made sure I would always sit up, sit up tight and make sure that I controlled the meetings, even when I didn't know what the hell I was doing. They thought I knew what I was doing because I acted confident. You know, my first day at work, they asked me to do the minutes. I timed the meeting. I found out the later the next day that the meeting, the minutes was the corporate records. And I told my secretary, I said, you got two choices. You can help me with this or you can tell everybody that I don't know what I'm doing. You tell everybody I don't know what I'm doing. I'll get fired and you'll have to work with one of those other guys that from Harvard, Bort and O'Yale. I'll be boring as hell. I guarantee you'll be really exciting if you keep me around. It was really exciting. But I, but I was the youngest senior VP in a very quick period of time. I was the most well-known person in Manhattan real estate within my first year of being in real estate and I didn't know anything. And that played over to me being an author. I got turned down by the, by over 300 agents and over 300 publishers. I got turned down by everybody. And the climb you ladder of success I was told was the first book to sell out on Amazon in 24 hours. I don't know if it wasn't that, but hit number one on Amazon in 24 hours. And I worked very, very hard to do that. My writing process is I sit down and I make the table of contents. I use a program now called, um, Shrivener. So it makes it easy. I could do my, yeah, I didn't use that at first. The way I, the way I come up with my table of contents is I go look for the dummies books. I figure they spent millions of dollars on marketing and figuring out what was important. So I look up the topics I want to write on, I go to the bookstore or go online. I look up these dummies books. I see what their table of contents is. I go look at maybe a half a dozen or a dozen other books that are similar to mine, look at their table of contents just to get some ideas. But I put my table of contents together. Then I start writing the chapters. I break the chapters up into basically three to 500 pay, three to 500 word articles. I write one article at a time. And by the time I'm done with all the articles, the book is done. Brilliant. And then do you ever go to search their reviews to see what you might want to be adding that people are complaining about? Yeah, I go look at the reviews. I do a lot of research, you know, to see what people want to see. And I have a pretty good idea what people want, because when I was a speaker, I would, I was one of the few speakers who hung around. I would hang around and talk to people. I like talking to people. So I'll talk to people, get their questions and, and get a good feel for that. What that's one of the things I like about being online, because you can get a lot of questions being in the online business now much easier than just being a regular traditional author. So yeah. And then as far as writer's block, you know, I know a lot of writers say that they get writer's block. I've never experienced that. That's one of the benefits of being a D student. I didn't know it. I didn't know it any better. So I knew that I didn't know I was supposed to have like writer's block. If I can't figure out something to write on, I'll Google what I'm looking for. And what I'll like, say it's motivation. I'll Google motivational, famous motivational quotes. I'll pick what I like. I put it on the top of my page and I start writing an article around that motivational quote. Sometimes I'll keep the quote in there and I obviously give credit to who it is. And then sometimes I'll take the quote out if it's, if it's a distraction to the reader. So if I'm getting any kind of a, if I don't have something to write on, that's all I do. I just look up a motivational quote. I write the article around it. Then I get to the next motivational quote. If I need it, just keep rolling and writing. I, I, I try to make sure I, I write a minimum of 2000 words a day. If it takes me two hours, it takes me two hours. If it takes me eight hours, it takes me eight hours, but I write 2000 words a day when I'm writing. When I write it, when I go on the contract for a book, I'm getting ready to go on the contract for a third hard book with a New York publisher. When I go on the contract for a book, they'll typically give you a deadline. What I do is I knock it out in 90 days. And then I've got the balance of the year to argue with the publisher, but what's important, it's not important, you know? So this way I meet my deadline and if, and the rest is on them because I say, no, a lot, they want me to take stuff out. No, no, no, no, no, no. Because I know it's important. What's not, I don't care what they think. They're not the marketer and then at the one who has the picture on the cover. So that's, that's kind of my process. It's pretty. No, but it's brilliant. I mean, there's no excuses in that anybody who follows that process has to end up with a book. And I mean, anyone gets on a topic, you've got something to say about it. You've got a chapter to write. Exactly. And I think that's, I think that it's as simple as that. I think people tend to make things a lot more complicated than they need to be. Yes. Absolutely. Our father used to teach people to go to the New York Times bestseller list and, you know, find, find your markets. But once you found a market or through the SRDS, he can have you go and read five of those books. Well, yeah. Yeah. And then people later on, I would tell people this and they'd say, well, you know, but how do you find something unique? I'm like, how do you read five books and not come up with your own unique thought or trick, you know, twist or trick on that? Yeah. So it's one of the keys to being an author is you need to be a voracious reader. I read a lot. So I did not have to go read five books on the subjects I write on because I've already read tens of thousands of books. So I don't need to do that. So, you know, if you want to be an author, you better damn well be a reader. Yeah. I think that's true. I think most 80% of successful people are voracious readers. It's funny because my copywriters all tend to play guitar for some reason. I have no idea why, but. Before my mouth. It makes it so funny. Yes. Yes. I play keyboards better than you play them. But, but yes, but when it comes to everybody, I've seen everyone that I look up to as a success is like their punishment in life would be if you took their books away. Yeah, I agree. Yeah. That that's how much books mean to feed them. They feed that curiosity and that just bringing into themselves all this stuff. So. I also want to point out something that brings it all together and makes you make sure that people are really getting the connection that you were mentioning earlier, John, which is, you know, when we talk about the physical aspect and, you know, mentally in the mental aspect, a lot of people don't understand how big that is a part of marketing and copyrighting. But I've been growing up in this business and whenever somebody comes out with their very first seminal work on, you know, being a good writer, like the bar on letters for my father and even John's rant, the very first thing they talk about is actually, you know, is getting your body and mind in shape because people do not understand how much your brain is a muscle. It's just like a muscle. It needs rest. It needs to be fed, you know, it needs input. It needs to be exercised as well. And the connection between mind and body is incredible. My father used to say, you recreate outside, which you carry around inside. So if your house is a mess, you know, your mind is a mess. But here's the one thing that I found out that was incredible that, you know, he kind of alluded to, but I figured out and started doing a lot with myself, which was you could it actually works in reverse. So if you go straighten up your closet, you're straightening up your mind at the same time. You know, if you're going to do exercise, you know, that's, you know, my, you know, when you mentioned walking, I'm a walk guy, you know, it's whenever I'm on, I have never, every time I've said, I've got to come up with an idea. I have to solve a problem or I have an issue. I always knew that solution was a walk away. Absolutely. I'm the same way. I agree with you a hundred percent. Yeah. And you know, the thing is, if you don't get that connection between them, you know, John, my favorite way John Carlton put it is he says, be a good animal. You know, get exercise, put the right nutrition in your body, take care of yourself, take care of some of your desires and your needs and, you know, balance that out. Because the next thing that, and we're going to talk, I want to ask you about this, that I find every single top marketer is into and very dialed into. And I also find that every single top copywriter is definitely into, which is, no matter what it is, they all have a routine. Yes. And the, you know, and Kevin talks about, you know, torturing them. I think the number one way to torture anybody is to take them out of their routine. You know, ask the guy, ask Kevin to get up at seven in the morning and write copy. Ask me to stay up at midnight and write it. You know, so tell me something about you, you know, can you share a little bit about your routine and what you find to be, you know, what, what, like, you know, how you carve out the time that you're doing? Because the one thing that I'm really picking up that you do really well, far better than I is management. You know, it is, you know, I mean, you obviously are not on the phone on 20, you know, two hours a day with your son and two hours a day with your assistant and two hours a day, you know, with all your affiliates and so forth. So how do you manage your time or your routine a little bit? I'm very, very focused on where my time gets spent. Even emails. I don't check emails. I have an assistant. I get on the phone with her every day at two o'clock from the West Coast. It's 11 o'clock, but it's two o'clock Eastern time. She runs through all my emails for me and responds to them, either as me or as somebody else. And that takes me 15 to 20 minutes. In fact, I was out in LA not long ago and I was walking to lunch with somebody. I said, hang on, I gotta get, I gotta take this call. I gotta do something. So I had my headset on, delete, delete, delete, respond. There's the responses, delete, delete, delete, forward, forward, forward 20 minutes. And the guy goes, what did you just do? I said, I just went through 386 emails. And that was the bottom line. And so I'm efficient. I try to be efficient. One of the things that I'm very cognizant of right now is my company has grown. My routine is changing. So I'm trying to refine one of the most important things for me to be focusing on at this point. So I make sure that I'm productive and efficient. I like to get up early. So I get up early. I have my morning routine that we spoke about before. I'm typically in the office or at work by seven or earlier. And I find my most productive and creative times is seven to 11. And but I also make sure I'm not sitting down or in one spot for more than 90 minutes at a time. Because I find that I get stuck at that point. So every 90 minutes I go for a walk. When I was in Manhattan real estate, we had an Australian secretary there. She called it Mr. Rowley's Walkabout. But every, every 90 minutes I stop. And I don't have the time. It's just innate. I know when it's time for me to stop. I take, I go for a walk. I go see my employees or I go get something to eat or do something like that. So I think it's really important to have a routine. Then in the afternoons, I typically will not take a phone call or an interview or anything until two o'clock. So I do those between two and five if at all possible. So my creative time is in the morning. 11 o'clock, I go to the gym with my staff. We all work out for an hour or so. And then I'll go grab lunch, you know, maybe do something that I need to do personally. And then I'm back in the office or back home again, working. And then at two o'clock, my phone calls, conference calls, things like that will take place. Even by yesterday when we spoke, it was probably at the two o'clock, I would guess, right? Yeah. I do, I do the exact same thing, which is, you know, there, I actually separate by days. Tuesday, Thursday's is my communications day where I respond back because I find people in the right mood and get them before their binds on the weekend. But in the morning, I don't, I try not to even check email. I try to be in communicado and just zoned in until, you know, maybe 10 o'clock. And then it's time to look check in on the rest of the world for me. And you go ahead. No, I separate my days as well. I work with Dan Sullivan and in 10 acts. And he has you doing free days, focused days and buffer days. So I do that. I'm just now at 55 years old learning how to do a free day. It's been a struggle. I have my first free day last week, believe it or not. I never disconnect. And when I came back, I'm rejuvenated and more creative. I need more free days. So I'm starting to do that. I'm going to start to do that more on a free days. This basically day off and I don't typically do that well. Um, and I'm starting to do more. Basically, I ended up doing a lot of buffer days lately because I'm cleaning up a lot of messes. We just launched a product. I've got new companies starting. So I'm starting to refine that a little bit. I actually had two phone calls today with two top, um, business guys that I noticed, see how they spent their day. So that's one thing I do. I don't try to reinvent the wheel. I contact the best of the best and find out what they do. And then I see if it fits into my life and it fits into my goals and desires, then I start doing the same thing. Yeah. And then, and then I think that's, that's kind of the other epiphany is that you, when you take the day off, you're working because it's vital to you to recharge yourself so that you're actually working at being productive by playing and, and forgetting and letting loose and everything else. So yeah, I always thought, you know, there's people that think they're working and then they think they're not working. And it's really that not working is just making you more effective when you go back. So that's difficult. Yeah. Work on time, you know, like for me, and I started off, I was an athlete, a very high level athlete. When I had my car accident, uh, it ended that I was in a walker for a few years and my dad got me a job as a janitor. So I basically started my career as an adult as a janitor. And, um, to me, work ethic was critical. So I always worked seven days a week, up before in the morning, worked until 11 o'clock at night. So now I'm at a point where I don't have to do that. I've been there for several years, um, but I do it. And so I struggle with that. I struggle with the time of I struggle with having a personal life, uh, because I love what I do. And that's, that's another thing I love coming to where I love. I absolutely love what I do. And, um, but in order for me to be effective and more creative, taking free time is critical, taking days off is critical. So I'm starting to discipline myself in that area now. Kevin and I were lucky because we never learned any work ethic. Our father was not, you know, the great thing is my dad used to tell this story and he told it several times and it happened a lot. He would go, he would have a protege or somebody and that would be with him and they would see him sign a new contractor, a new deal to write an ad. And then, um, he would come back and, uh, you know, they go, and they'd go boating and got to movies and hang out and everything. And the ad would be done and we do in 30 days and about a week before the ad was due, my dad would get up in the morning and tell you to, you know, sit down on the couch right there. He would sit at the kitchen table and I think Kevin still has this table, but he'd sit there and he would write out the promotion. Right. And he'd say, okay, D and he'd say, time me, right? You know, some of the, the protege would time them and say, okay, that took you, you know, 30 minutes to write and everything. He goes, no, that took me and he goes, I didn't take me 30 minutes to write. It took me 30 days and 30 minutes to write. And he said, now do you believe that? And he'd go, yeah, and he goes, well, that's still wrong. It took me 30 years, 30 days and three minutes to write. But Kevin and I really learned quite early from our dad growing up and watching him that he who was working hardest wasn't the key player all the time and that he who was working, you know, that, you know, so a lot of people who really not get that quality work isn't, you know, and being, being good at marketing and everything else isn't putting in 40 hours a week. It's putting in, if you, you can end up putting in 60 or 80, but guy, there are guys who would, you know, the strategy, their head is in the right place. They've spent a lot of time exercising and clearing it. They're half an hour in the morning is equivalent to somebody else's two hours. I agree with you a hundred percent. I get more done by nine o'clock in the morning than the average guy gets done full week. Yeah. And, and, and it's so, so people really need to not equate sitting at the desk and typing with marketing and copyrighting. No, not unless you want to work for somebody else and get paid by the hour. Yeah. And copywriting, that's not where the copy's done. You know, the copywriting is written. Um, it's curiosity. It's going out there and experiencing the world and having, you know, and being able to, you know, see these angles that, you know, mixing one thing up with another and stuff like that. It is, it is about the, it's, there's so much about the mental preparation in putting yourself in the right frame of mind. And so everything you were talking about with, you know, um, you know, not just getting your mind set up and straight, but bringing up the, the other books and looking at the table of contents, you've primed your mind to accept it, to take all of that and do something really good with it. Whereas somebody who's bleary eyed from typing or working for 60 hours to set something up, they can't even see what they're looking at. It's just words on the page that are all like the sea of things. Well, there's a big difference between being an author and being a copywriter. I'm an author. You guys are copywriters. John Benson, John Colton, their copywriters, your dad, you're the best copywriters, you guys have sick minds, man. You guys can see things that nobody else sees. And that's how you come up with these hooks. And for me, I wish I had that, you know, I'd love to be able to look beyond the, you know, it's like almost being like in the movie, the metrics where you guys like, you know, kind of like, you know, take the green pillow of a bread pill. And when I'm with John Colton, I'm careful not to take anything. But John, if you're listening to this, I'm not joking. But I love talking to you guys. You can look, you can look beyond the natural. You can look beyond what everybody else is seeing and find something that's going to compel somebody to buy and still do it ethically, which to me is phenomenal. And it goes back to what I was saying yesterday, which was you look for passion, you look for energy, you look for things that really grab people's attention. You know, our dad went around and screwed with people in person. So he knew exactly how to push their buttons and do it remotely with words. And it's a, and, you know, we get our best, you know, ideas and everything. Also talking with people like you. I remember I was having a conversation with a lot and we were talking and we were talking about the boring letters and I said, Oh man, thank God for the boring letters. You know, if my dad didn't write those letters, I would have to write them down. My dad didn't write those letters and tell the whole world, look, I'm teaching my son at 15 how to write copy. Nobody would believe this incredible education I got. And I said, thank God my dad went to prison. I love, I love that book, by the way. Yeah. And he started, well, he started laughing and we were like, okay, yeah, that's got to be another subject lie. Right. It worked and grabbed everybody's attention. A good, a good point of that book for anybody who's, for anybody who's listening, who wants to be an author is the nice thing about the boring letters, you can read a page or two and there's a break. You can kind of close it and then you can come back the next day and not losing anything because they're, they're very short. A lot of authors have a tendency to kind of ramble on and go too long. I, when I write a book, I always want to make sure there's a breaking point. Every two pages. So they can read two pages, put a business card or flap it all, whatever they're going to do, and then they can pick up the next day without losing it. And it sounds like you're talking about having like a complete idea. Exactly. A complete idea. And then kind of like, it doesn't need to be the end of the chapter, but like have like a little headline. And when they come back to that little headline, I write, my books have headlines for lack of a better word, but every headline comes to another complete idea. And usually they don't go more than four to 500 words and then they can break. So it gives the reader an opportunity. Okay. I don't, I don't, I really can't read anymore today. And they're not overwhelmed with what they're reading and they can probably, you know, I feel they get a lot more out of it. For everybody to know the subtle differences between content and, and, um, marketing, writing copy. And let me tell you something. They're very close together. You're still keeping some, you know, with copywriting, you're gripping and trying to keep their attention and keep on point. It's about brevity and so forth. There are subtle differences like in marketing, you know, everybody's looking for a place to feel comfortable to stop reading. Okay. But you don't give it to them and copy, you know, you, you know, as soon as you give them a complete thought, you, you tail it with something that makes them want to, you know, feel like they have to read the next line until they're salivating for your product. But the, the idea of how you're grabbing their attention using subhead, um, you know, not, uh, you know, I relief as I father put it so that, you know, you're not looking at a legal disclaimer. I always like legal disclaimers because they're the exact opposite. They are designed to not be read. So you just do the exact opposite of what's in a legal disclaimer. So they are all caps. They're all one big, long paragraph. They're all boring, really long sentences, no subheads, no pictures. You know, they're designed not to be read. And, um, you know, you do the opposite. But so a lot of these, those things are all, they're going to be uniform. Excuse me. Those, those strategies are something that you're going to use for content and for copy because after you write your copy, we're big proponents of obviously selling info products. And when you're writing info products, the difference is, is you're giving it your, it's the, it's the, the focus is the same thing. It's the reader. What does the reader need? And what does the reader want to make them understand that this is something that'll be at value to their life? That's the copy part. When you do the content, it's the same question. What's going to keep their attention? What's going to add value to their life? And there's, and so forth. The rest of you at all. Yeah. You know what? I, I, we can, Kevin and I put, you know, I don't know where we picked up, but we call it the so wet test, you know, and what'll happen is it's so easy to get wrapped up into thinking that, you know, oh, everybody's loving this. And I'll tell them, Hey, you know, my name's Bond and I was a graduate and I got a, you know, degree in international business administration and so forth. And Kevin and I, one time we ran this test and it was a three way split. And he did everything the exact same, you know, or nothing exact same. We had completely different pieces, but we had different spelling pieces and everything, but we ran a long version and a shortened version. And the shortened version was just all I did was go through it and say, okay, you know, people will cut this, you know, not care about it. And it's hard to do that for some people, but that's an important key for good writing is you, when you go and don't do this, don't think about this at all while you're writing. Go ahead and write everything out. You know, this all, what you cut out is in the editing process. Absolutely. You know, writing a book is not writing, writing a book is editing. Yes. Now, earlier on, we were talking about, you know, focusing on what the focus on. When I was speaking to traffic and conversion summit, you know, there's so many things to learn. And when I was on stage, the thing I, they wanted me to speak on how to build an affiliate army. When I first started, I focused on one thing was affiliates. Now we do other things. But I focused on affiliates. And for anybody listening out there, pick one thing to focus on. If you're going to buy media, buy media, if you're going to focus on affiliates, focus on affiliates, master that, stop making a legitimate income with that, and then go on to the next thing. I know there's a lot of copywriters that will be on this because of you guys. And there is, from the horse's mouth, my biggest hole in my business is copywriting. I could probably be a very good copywriter if I focused on it. I just don't feel, I don't feel it's one of my core competencies. I don't think it's, I don't think it's worth my effort focusing, especially since I have one of the best in the world writing for me, which is John Benson. But if John didn't write for me, that would be, I would, that's a huge hole in my business. So for anybody out there who's a good copywriter or wants to be a good copywriter, guys like me are constantly looking for legitimate, hard working copywriters that can, that can convert. And we don't mind paying a lot of money for it because if we have it, like old school, the body's been making me a lot of money for three and a half years. And it's the gift that keeps on giving me and it hasn't stopped. It's still in the top five on Clickbank. John, the people who are listening, the fact that they're part of this program would indicate that they're just getting started or they're trying to learn something, they're trying to improve something. And you mentioned something, that process that you've gone through where you take a day for yourself. So in the evolution of business, you know, the psychology and the mentality that gets you to from zero to one million dollars is a particular mindset. But it has to be different when you go from one to five and so on. You know, you're learning all the time. So if you were to give the people who are starting off now or the people who are listening to this, other than just get started, what were those were the milestones along the way where you thought that that's a good point? That's a good lesson I just learned or taught myself or that's something I have to take note of. When I started doing this, I was, this is the God's honest truth. I was standing on my back, back in a conversation with John Benson. My house was on the foreclosure. I hadn't owned the car in three years. I lost everything. Um, and I'm thinking to myself, oh my God, I hope this is not another one of these, you know, this nothing's going to happen. And I remember asking John, how long is it going to take before we make money? Because as soon as we launch, I didn't even know what launching meant. My goal and my prayer was God, just let me make $500 a week so we could eat. And we made a lot more than that the first month out of the blocks. And there were three partners in this number I will give. There were three partners, first number out of the blocks without monetizing the list just on the sale of old school new body. Each of us made $10,000 the first month. And that was only with a couple of affiliates promoting it. We didn't do a big launch. We did a rolling launch because I didn't know anybody. So, um, I think the thing is set, set goals. Yeah. Set milestones. In my case, I didn't know what the goals were because I didn't know the business at all. So my goal was 500 a week. And then when I saw that we could make real money at it, then all of a sudden, you know, I went to millions. I don't, I didn't have an in between phase. You know, I'm, I'm go big or go home. So, you know, I saw we were going to do it. All 100% of my effort went into this. I didn't do any more public speaking. I wasn't writing hard cover books and I've been asked to do some co-op cover books. Turn that down for the last three years. I'm getting ready to do another one. But for the last three years, I, I focused on making this business a big business and we're there. So now I can go on to other things. So yeah, set goals. Then once you hit that goal, you know, it has to be comfortable, but uncomfortable. You know, I started my supplement company. Everybody's like, you know, you know, you got your nuts. You're real, because I could have white labeled something. You're creating your own thing. This is going to cost hundreds of thousands of dollars, which it did cost me a lot of money to do this. But the downside for me at this point in my life is. Okay. So I've got a, you know, thousands of dollars worth of hundreds of thousands of dollars worth of supplements I'll use. The downside for me is I've got something that I created that I love. Thank God it's selling well. And you know, our reorders are through the roof. People love the product, but you set a goal and work hard towards the goal and focus on one thing. Don't focus on 5,000 things. Focus on one thing and make it successful. Most of the people, especially the people starting out out there, they're focusing on the wrong things. They're trying to get affiliates to promote for something that's not even promoting, that's not even converting. So focus on, focus on one thing at a time, get good at it and then move on to the other thing. And yes, set goals, you know, set goals and the goal can be having one affiliate promote for you. Um, for me, that was, I was really lucky in the beginning where I had some of the top affiliates I'd became friends with. So it worked out well. Um, a lot of that is, Bob, what you were saying, you know, your dad said it takes 30 years and 30 days to write that, you know, I'm an overnight success. After 35 years of busting my ass. So, um, it took 35 years to make these three years work. So it didn't just happen overnight. It didn't just happen in one month of launching and click bank. My whole life has been leading to this. I want to highlight one special thing that you mentioned, John, which is do something you love, you know, the thing is in marketing, you get a choice here and what you go on to sell. And it used to be that if you were in print or you were in something, you had to go for one of these wide niches, you know, you had to go for something that was weight loss, finance, something that a huge chunk of the portion of the population was into. And you, that's still a great place to go. Yes. But you can now make money selling cat TRs because the internet allows you to find the five people on the earth that are willing to do that. But the thing is when you sell something you love, you're writing about something you love, you're more interested when you're researching it. So you get more power because the, you know, the power in your marketing is in research, you know, when you're stuck talking to people and doing business, you're talking about something you love. So that, that's also a very big key that I see with a lot of the marketers. Choose. Don't say what's going to be just a lucrative area to jump into. Jump into an area that where, you know, your attention span, that, that perfect attitude, which is if this doesn't work out, I got $100,000 worth of supplements. You know, if this, you know, if this doesn't work out, Kevin, I'm just taking the course that we've always wanted to take. I agree. And I, I do see some of the markers that get into this business only for the money and they don't list. So then, and there's another component. Um, I only will work with marketers that are ethical and we review every product, I don't review it, but somebody in my staff reviews every product that we promote. Now, do I agree 100% with the philosophy of certain things? No, uh, I own a product, the Pilates product with Sylvia Fevella. I'm partners on that. I was partners with her before I ever tried Pilates. Wasn't a huge believer in it. I didn't really know much about it, but I trusted her and I liked her and she was ethical and moral. Um, a couple of weeks ago, I was out in California. I was at her studio and I got roped into doing a Pilates thing. And I woke up the next morning. I thought I had the flu because my abs are hurting. So now I'm a belief, now I'm a believer in Pilates, but before I was a believer in Pilates, I was a believer in the person who created the product. And so we don't promote anything we don't, we don't believe in, even though philosophies may be a little bit different than what I use. My goal is to make sure that we're bringing something from an ethical expert to all lists. And that's, that's another thing is important. You can burn your list out real quick. We've got a huge list and our attrition rate, um, is very, very low. And we walk into the list, but what we also do is we bring me to the list. You know, I'm on the Huffington Post, I'm on TV. We, you know, we do certain things. So we make sure that they know who I am. Also another thing that I found very useful is in, um, old school in the body and basically any product that I'll promote, I have audios. I typically interview people and we'll have audios on old school in the body. We've got 20 interviews with top experts. And the feedback we get is all school in the body is really good, but John's interviews are changing my life. So they listen to the interviews. When they get an email from me, they think they know me. Yes. Because they've listened to me. They've heard me. They've seen me on TV. They've seen my videos. They've listened to my interviews. They listened to my audios. They feel like they know me. I'm not just the guy sitting at home in my underwear. I was going to say, I was going to leave it, go on with that. But I'm not just the guy sitting at home in my underwear, marketing, sending out emails. I'm actually out in the real world. I'm a legitimate expert. And I hate that word expert because I always feel like I'm learning and learning and learning, but in the eyes of them on an expert. And, um, but by letting them hear my voice, by letting them hear me and see me, they feel like they know me. So when they receive my emails, they feel like they're getting an email. A lot of times my customer service will get an email back. It's like they're writing back to a friend. They think I'm getting it. But yeah, I don't see those. But if somebody sends a, if somebody sends a phone number through, I will call the people. I'll get on the phone and I'll call the people because I think if they put the effort into putting a phone number in there, I'll call them. So I'll spend, you know, a couple of times a month making phone calls and they really shook up when they get me, but I'll spend an hour on the phone with somebody because I think it's important that they, that, you know, I reach out to them. If it all possible. So I think, you know, more than just being a marketer, they want to see somebody who's actually a human being. And I think that's more important than good market. It's not more important than good marketing. You need to make money. You make the marketing through the marketing, through the marketing. You make the money through the market, but you keep them on your list by letting them know that you're somebody real. You're not just a cartoon character on a header of an email that comes out. Good point. Good point. That's my philosophy. I'm not a, I'm not a top copywriter, but my, that's my, that's my philosophy. It's a pretty good one too.
